<p class="recon-summary">This extension comes with an experimental version of faceted search for Semantic MediaWiki. It is a customisable tool that uses fixed filters to let users narrow down search results. You can enable it using a parser function <code>#recon-faceted-search</code>, which takes most of its instructions from a user-provided configuration profile in JSON. For any API-based facet, it relies on reconciliation profiles whose page IDs only need to be referenced in a faceted search profile.</p>

== Parser function <code>#recon-faceted-search</code> ==
To add faceted search to a wiki page, add an instance of the <code>#recon-faceted-search</code> parser function : 

<pre>
{{#recon-faceted-search:
|profile= // Mandatory
|output= / ask, template or basic
|resultformats= // only if output=template

// if output=ask
|format= // result format
|template=
|limit=
|sort=
|order=
|valuesep=
// and other #ask-specific parameters

|defaultfilters=
|maxpages=
|scrollmargintop=
|updateurl=
|runonload=
|debug=
}}
</pre>

<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>profile</code></div>
<div>Pagename of your config profile (mandatory).</div>
</div>

<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>output</code></div>
<div>What output method to use for your query results. You can decide between three options :
* (1) 'ask' (default) - create an <code>#ask</code> query and have that parsed.
* (2) 'template' - leave it up to a wiki template to run queries and visualise data. The template just receives the query condition along with other dynamic data such as 'offset' and 'sort'. More advanced but also more flexible and useful, for instance, if you want to pair two different visualisations.
* (3) 'basic' - a minimal presentation will be used instead.</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>resultformats</code></div>
<div>Used exclusively with output=template to establish which ResourceLoader modules must be loaded. See relevant section below.</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>template</code></div>
<div>If output=ask, the wiki template to be used to format query results; if output=template, the wiki template to be used for handling the query.</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>valuesep</code></div>
<div>The delimiter for property values in an #ask query. Default: <code>;</code></div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>sort</code></div>
<div>Optionally, the property by which to sort the #ask query. SMW's default is to sort by page sortkey or pagename.
</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>order</code></div>
<div>asc/ascending (SMW's default) or desc/descending</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>limit</code></div>
<div>(default: 10) - maximum number of results to show at a time.</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div>...</div>
<div>With output=ask, you can use any other <code>#ask</code> parameter.</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>defaultfilters</code></div>
<div>Optionally, a list of preset filters to be used when the page loads. The list separator is three consecutive hyphens (<code>---</code>) and each filter should be given in the format <code><nowiki><name here>=value</nowiki></code>, where the first part corresponds to the <code>name</code> of the facet. To list multiple values for the multiselect input, use the value of <code>valuesep</code> as your separator. For example: <pre>defaultfilters=term=cocktail --- ingredients=maraschino cherry;pineapple juice</pre></div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>maxpages</code></div>
<div>(default: 5) - maximum number of pages to show in the pagination widget, with the possible exception of a first and last number.</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>scrollmargintop</code></div>
<div>When you navigate to a new result batch using the pagination widget at the bottom of your search results, the page will automatically scroll up to the top. If your site happens to use a fixed header, the unfortunate side-effect is that the very top of the widget will then be buried under the header. To remedy this, set 'scrollmargintop' to the height of your header, including a unit such as 'px' or 'em'.</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>updateurl</code></div>
<div>By default, the URL remains clean and untouched whenever a new query is run. You can also enable faceted navigation or bookmarking for reference and sharing. Filter values will be pre-loaded when a user later visits a URL that's been enriched with filters. To enable it, set <code>updateurl</code> to one of the following values:
* 'search' - adds the filter parameters to the query part of the URL, after the question mark (<code>?</code>).
* 'fragment' - adds the filter parameters to the URI fragment, after the hash (<code>#</code>). Consider this option if you think that on your wiki, the traditional query option ('search') is prone to abuse by crawlers. For additional guidance on this topic, [https://developers.google.com/crawling/docs/faceted-navigation#prevent-crawling-of-faceted-navigation-urls see these recommendations by Google].
Because there is only one URL at a time, you should not enable this feature for more than a single instance of the parser function on the page.
</div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>runonload</code></div>
<div>Whether the query should be run and its results shown when the page loads. This is the default behaviour. If instead you want to wait for the user to interact, you can opt out by setting <code>runonload=false</code>. </div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>addfilterstouserparam</code></div>
<div>Either 'true' or 'false' (default). If output=ask, determines whether filters used should be sent to the <code>userparam</code> parameter of the #ask query. The string that this parameter will receive is delimited and formatted in the same way as is done for <code>defaultfilters</code> above: again <code> --- </code> to separate the filters and the value of <code>valuesep</code> to separate the filter values. </div>
</div>
<div class="recon-data-item">
<div><code>debug</code></div>
<div>If set to "true", some additional info about your query and query results will be shown to help you debug any possible issues.</div>
</div>

=== Result formats and ResourceLoader modules ===
Result formats such as 'plainlist' (with template) and 'table' or 'broadtable' should be usable out of the box. To be fully functional, many more advanced result formats require one or multiple ResourceLoader modules to be loaded. Unfortunately, many result formats have not been written to support deferred DOM updates by third-party tools such as ours. Known exceptions are those that have up set up a listener (through the 'wikipage.content' hook) in their code:

* <code>leaflet</code>, part of the Maps extension
* <code>iiif-annotation-gallery</code>, part of the IIIF extension
* <code>iiif-canvas-viewer</code>, also part of the IIIF extension

Unfortunately, <code>gallery</code> is only partially supported, without any of the lightbox functionality.

If you are using 'template' as your 'output' mode and your template uses any result format, then add a comma-separated list of these formats to '''resultformats'''. For instance, if you add 'leaflet', this extension makes sure the required ResourceLoader module is available in time for the map to initialise.

=== Examples ===

;ask
<pre>{{#recon-faceted-search:
|profile=Recon:FacetedSearch/Recipes
|output=ask
|format=plainlist
|template=Show recipe
|link=none
|named args=true
|valuesep=;
|limit=10
|maxpages=5
}}</pre>

;template
<pre>{{#recon-faceted-search:
|profile=Recon:FacetedSearch/Recipes
|output=template
|template=RecipeSearch
|resultformats=leaflet
|maxpages=5
}}</pre>

;basic
<pre>{{#recon-faceted-search:
|output=basic
|profile=Recon:FacetedSearch/Recipes
|maxpages=5
}}</pre>

=== Initialisation options ===
==== Preset filters ====
There are two ways in which you can load the interface with preset filters.
# through <code>updateurl</code> : gets filters from the URL's query string or fragment.
# through <code>defaultfilters</code>: gets filters through the parser function.

Both options are explained in more detail above. If these options are used together, <code>updateurl</code> takes precedence over <code>defaultfilters</code>. If no URL filters are found, and only then, an additional check for the presence of default filters will be made before updating the query.

==== Run query on page load ====
When the page loads, the default behaviour is to run the query and show its results, with or without predefined filters. You can opt out by setting <code>runonload=false</code> in the parser function.
